qa.overall_assessment: The BA spec correctly identifies a real privilege escalation (schema mismatch between writer and matcher). The root cause is confirmed by live code inspection and runtime verification. However, BA missed two pre-existing writer-side bugs that make the fix incomplete and the ACs untestable via the real /allow user path: (1) bare '/allow Write /path' silently strips the path as a comment, and (2) '_looks_regex()' classifies any pattern containing '.' as a regex, causing the sentinel write to be skipped entirely — which means the AC1/AC2 target paths (/tmp/allowed.json) can NEVER produce a sentinel via '/allow --tool'. The fix approach to the matcher is correct and necessary. The fix approach to the writer is also correct in principle — but incomplete unless the two writer-path defects are addressed in scope. Without fixing these, the new 'target' field in the writer will never be reached for the majority of real-world file paths (those with extensions).

issues.title: BA missed: bare '/allow Write /path' silently treats path as comment — writer emits bare wildcard
issues.detail: At hooks/userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:90-97, when the user runs '/allow Write /tmp/specific-file.json', the parser splits on the first bare token ('Write') as the pattern and treats '/tmp/specific-file.json' as a comment. The sentinel writer at lines 199-204 then gets pattern='Write' (no rest), so it emits {'op':'Write'} — a bare wildcard. Runtime verified: printf '{"prompt":"/allow Write /tmp/specific-file.json",...}' | hooks/userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh produces sentinel {'allowed_operations': [{'op': 'Write'}]}. This means the documented scoped-Write intent is silently lost. The writer fix (emit 'target' instead of 'args_contain') never fires because 'rest' is empty when the user omits '--tool'.
issues.evidence: Live execution output: '[allow] Grant recorded: pattern='Write' is_regex=false comment='/tmp/specific-file.json'. Sentinel: {"op":"Write"} — bare wildcard, not scoped.'
issues.proposed_fix: The writer must either: (A) special-case the bare 'Write <path>' form by detecting this pattern in the sentinel-write block and emitting 'target': path, or (B) update all /allow documentation and ACs to require '/allow --tool "Write <path>"' as the ONLY supported scoped syntax, with a clear user-visible error or warning when the bare form is used. BA must choose one and encode it as an explicit requirement. The fix approach ('writer emits target instead of args_contain') is correct only if the user input reaches the writer with 'rest' populated — which requires --tool flag usage.

issues.title: BA missed: '_looks_regex()' classifies any path with dots as regex — sentinel write skipped, AC1/AC2 unreachable
issues.detail: At hooks/userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:81-88, '_looks_regex()' checks for regex metacharacters including '.' (the dot). File paths with extensions (e.g. '/tmp/allowed.json', '/tmp/foo.py', '/root/file.sh') contain a dot, so IS_REGEX is set to 'true'. The sentinel write block at lines 180-182 exits immediately when IS_REGEX='true'. Runtime verified: '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/allowed.json"' (the exact path used in AC1 and AC2) produces IS_REGEX=true and NO sentinel file. This means: (1) the writer fix is entirely unreachable for the most common real-world file paths, (2) AC1 and AC2 test conditions ('/tmp/allowed.json') cannot be reproduced via the /allow user path — only via a directly hand-crafted sentinel. The ACs test the matcher in isolation but cannot be reached through the actual user workflow for typical filenames.
issues.evidence: Live execution output: '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/allowed.json"' → '[allow] Grant recorded: pattern='Write /tmp/allowed.json' is_regex=true' → Sentinel: MISSING. Dotless path '/tmp/allowed' works: produces sentinel with args_contain=['/tmp/allowed'].
issues.proposed_fix: The '--tool' flag MUST force is_regex=false unconditionally, regardless of '_looks_regex()' output. When the user explicitly uses '--tool', they are declaring a literal tool/path name — the auto-regex heuristic should not override an explicit literal declaration. The fix at hooks/userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:86-88 should be: 'if flag_pattern is not None: pattern = flag_pattern; is_regex = False  # --tool always literal'. BA must add this to the scope of the fix and add an AC verifying that '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/file.json"' produces a sentinel (not regex-skipped). This fix is a prerequisite for the writer fix to have any real-world effect.

issues.title: BA missed: ACs are matcher-only and cannot be reached via the real /allow writer path for typical filenames
issues.detail: All 6 ACs test match_sentinel_grant_for_write() with hand-crafted sentinel dictionaries. None of the ACs test the full stack: user types '/allow --tool "Write /path"' → writer emits sentinel → guard reads sentinel → match/no-match. Due to CRITICAL-2 (regex skip for dotted paths), the ACs for AC1 and AC2 using '/tmp/allowed.json' cannot be triggered via the real /allow command — only via hand-crafting the sentinel. This means the fix could pass all ACs while the real user experience remains broken for any file with an extension.
issues.proposed_fix: Add at least one end-to-end AC that invokes hooks/userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh with a realistic '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/file.json"' input, checks that a sentinel with target='/tmp/file.json' is written, then invokes match_sentinel_grant_for_write and verifies it allows the named file and denies a different file. This requires the '--tool literal' fix from CRITICAL-2 to land first.

issues.title: BA scope excludes two writer-path bugs that are co-required for the fix to work end-to-end
issues.detail: The BA spec correctly identifies the sentinel writer (userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh) as an affected file and prescribes a fix (replace args_contain with target). However, the two preconditions that must hold for any path-scoped Write grant to actually produce a sentinel with a 'target' field — (A) the '--tool' flag must produce a literal pattern, and (B) the resulting pattern must not be classified as a regex — are both broken and excluded from scope. The fix as scoped lands the correct matcher logic and the correct writer assignment, but neither can be exercised via the real user path because the sentinel is never written with an extension path.
issues.proposed_fix: Expand scope to include: (1) fixing '--tool' to always produce is_regex=false; (2) add a writer-level integration test. These are NOT optional hardening — they are necessary for the fix to be reachable. Without them, the privilege escalation persists for all files with extensions, which is the majority of real-world targets.

issues.title: Stale docstring in match_sentinel_grant_for_write still describes the buggy wildcard semantic
issues.detail: hooks/lib/allowlist.py lines 488-492 document 'If entry[target] is absent or null: wildcard — matches any target_path.' This is precisely the vulnerability being fixed. After the fix, this comment must be updated to reflect the corrected behavior: absent target AND absent args_contain = intentional wildcard; target present = exact equality; args_contain present = exact equality legacy fallback.
issues.proposed_fix: Update the docstring as part of the fix. BA spec Technical Hints should include this as an explicit change (currently only hinted at via the fix description, not called out as a required docstring update). Codex OBSERVATION_ONLY finding #6.

root_cause_verification.rationale: The schema mismatch (writer emits args_contain, matcher checks target, resulting in unconditional None-match wildcard) is confirmed by direct code inspection and live runtime execution. This is a real privilege escalation. The BA's root cause description is accurate.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: the sentinel produced has args_contain=["/some/path"] but NO target field
success_criteria_results.verification_method: Code inspection of test_allowlist_consolidation.py lines 534-653 and userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh lines 199-204
success_criteria_results.details: AC5 correctly prescribes a regression test for the args_contain exploit shape. This AC is sound. However, the precondition for the writer fix to produce a 'target' field (instead of args_contain) for real-world paths requires CRITICAL-2 fix first. AC5 tests the matcher in isolation, which is correct for that AC scope.
success_criteria_results.evidence: Live runtime confirms writer produces args_contain for non-dotted paths and skips sentinel write entirely for dotted paths.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: The matcher then sees no target and grants Write access to ANY file
success_criteria_results.criterion: AC2 — scoped grant blocks any file other than the named one
success_criteria_results.details: AC2 is correctly formulated and tests the right matcher behavior. However, AC2 cannot be reached through the real /allow user path for any file with an extension (like /tmp/allowed.json) because the sentinel is never written due to CRITICAL-2. The AC can only pass if the sentinel is hand-crafted directly.
success_criteria_results.evidence: Live execution: '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/allowed.json"' produces no sentinel. AC2 test would require hand-crafting the sentinel file, not testing the real user flow.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: the writer MUST generate a 'target' field for Write operations (not just args_contain)
success_criteria_results.criterion: AC1 + AC2 — verified via writer + matcher together
success_criteria_results.details: BA's requirement R3 is correct and necessary. The writer fix (emit 'target' instead of 'args_contain') is the right change. However, the fix is unreachable for any dotted file path because IS_REGEX=true causes the sentinel write to be skipped entirely. The writer code at lines 199-204 can only be reached when IS_REGEX=false, which requires a dotless filename.
success_criteria_results.evidence: Live execution confirms: '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/allowed.json"' → IS_REGEX=true → sentinel write skipped → no sentinel produced.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: the matcher MUST validate target strictly (reject when target absent and file path does not match args_contain)
success_criteria_results.criterion: AC4 — legacy args_contain exact equality; AC6 — intentional bare wildcard preserved
success_criteria_results.verification_method: Code inspection of allowlist.py:484-524 and test class TestMatchSentinelGrantForWrite
success_criteria_results.details: AC4 and AC6 correctly prescribe matcher behavior for legacy args_contain sentinel and bare wildcard sentinel respectively. These ACs test the matcher in isolation with hand-crafted sentinels and are sound. The matcher fix logic (key-presence branching, exact equality for args_contain fallback, bare wildcard only for no-target no-args_contain) is correctly specified.
success_criteria_results.evidence: Existing test test_targetless_wildcard_matches_any_path at line 634 validates AC6. AC4 is a new test case not yet present, correctly identified as missing by BA.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: Posttool correctly consumes the grant after Write use
success_criteria_results.criterion: AC3 — posttool unlinks sentinel after Write to named file
success_criteria_results.details: AC3 is correctly formulated. The posttool at line 163 calls match_sentinel_grant_for_write — the matcher fix implicitly fixes posttool behavior. The existing posttool consume chain (candidates list, malformed fallback) is correct and does not need changes.
success_criteria_results.evidence: posttool-allowlist-consume.py:163 confirmed to call match_sentinel_grant_for_write(candidate, session_id, file_path).

ba_validation_objections.title: BA did not investigate the full writer path: bare '/allow Write /path' produces a wildcard sentinel, not a scoped one
ba_validation_objections.evidence: Live execution: '/allow Write /tmp/specific-file.json' → pattern='Write', is_regex=false, comment='/tmp/specific-file.json' → sentinel {'op':'Write'} (bare wildcard). The path is discarded as a comment.
ba_validation_objections.impact: The fix instruction 'writer emits target instead of args_contain' is never reached for bare /allow syntax. Users following the obvious syntax get a wildcard grant, not a scoped one.
ba_validation_objections.required_ba_action: Investigate and document which /allow syntax reliably produces a path-scoped sentinel. Update Requirements and ACs to match the chosen supported syntax. Add explicit M-requirement for the '--tool-forces-literal' fix or the bare-syntax special-case.

ba_validation_objections.title: BA did not investigate _looks_regex() interaction with '--tool' flag — dotted paths skip sentinel write entirely
ba_validation_objections.evidence: Source: userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:86-88 — when flag_pattern is set, is_regex = _looks_regex(flag_pattern) is still called. _looks_regex() returns True for any string containing '.'. Lines 180-182: 'if [ "$IS_REGEX" = "true" ]; then exit 0; fi' skips sentinel write. Live execution confirms '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/allowed.json"' produces IS_REGEX=true and no sentinel.
ba_validation_objections.impact: The writer fix is unreachable for any real-world filename with an extension (.json, .py, .sh, .txt, etc.). The majority of real /allow Write use cases never produce a sentinel at all. Privilege escalation persists for all these paths even after the fix.
ba_validation_objections.required_ba_action: Add to scope: fix '--tool' flag to force is_regex=false (the --tool flag is an explicit literal declaration; _looks_regex() must not override it). Add AC verifying '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/file.json"' writes a sentinel with target='/tmp/file.json'.

ba_validation_objections.title: ACs are matcher-only and cannot be reached via real /allow user path for typical filenames
ba_validation_objections.evidence: AC1 and AC2 use '/tmp/allowed.json' which contains '.'. This path triggers IS_REGEX=true and skips sentinel write. The ACs can only pass with a hand-crafted sentinel, not via the actual /allow workflow.
ba_validation_objections.impact: ACs can be made to pass by testing the matcher in isolation while the real user experience remains broken. The fix would be verified against an artificial test condition, not the production-shaped data the actual user encounters.
ba_validation_objections.required_ba_action: Add at least one end-to-end AC that invokes userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh and verifies the full stack produces a scoped sentinel and correct guard behavior. This AC requires the CRITICAL-2 fix (--tool forces literal) to land first.

codex_consult.feedback_summary: Codex identified 5 blockers and 1 observation: (1) bare '/allow Write /path' produces wildcard sentinel (path treated as comment); (2) '--tool' with dotted paths triggers regex classification and skips sentinel write entirely; (3) ACs are matcher-heavy and cannot be reached via real writer path; (4) legacy args_contain fallback must be type/key strict with exclusive branches; (5) posttool consume implicit fix claim needs clarification. One observation: stale docstring describes the bug, not the fix.
codex_consult.feedback_incorporated: Codex blockers 1, 2, and 3 independently verified via live runtime execution and confirmed as genuine critical findings. Finding 2 is particularly severe: '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/allowed.json"' (the exact AC1/AC2 path) produces no sentinel at all due to _looks_regex() treating '.' as a regex metachar. Codex finding 4 (exclusive branches) is already correctly addressed by BA's Technical Hints and M2/M3. Codex finding 5 (posttool) is adequately addressed by AC3 and the matcher implicit-fix chain. Observation 6 (docstring) escalated to MINOR-1 finding. Codex blockers 1 and 2 escalated to CRITICAL-1 and CRITICAL-2 in this report. Codex blocker 3 escalated to MAJOR-1.

qa.approval_notes: REJECTED. The BA spec correctly identifies the real privilege escalation and the matcher fix is sound. However, BA missed two critical pre-existing writer-path defects that make the fix non-functional for the majority of real-world file paths: (1) bare '/allow Write /path' silently discards the path as a comment and emits a wildcard sentinel; (2) '_looks_regex()' classifies any filename containing '.' (the most common case: .json, .py, .sh) as a regex, causing the sentinel write to be skipped entirely. Because of defect (2), the AC1 and AC2 test scenarios ('/tmp/allowed.json') cannot be produced via the real /allow user path — only via hand-crafting. The fix must be expanded to include: (a) forcing '--tool' flag to set is_regex=false unconditionally, and (b) either special-casing bare 'Write <path>' parsing or documenting clearly that --tool is required. BA must re-analyze, update scope, and update ACs to include an end-to-end test that exercises the full writer-to-guard stack with a realistic dotted filename.

failed_criteria: Writer fix is unreachable for any filename with an extension (.json, .py, .sh, etc.) because _looks_regex() classifies the pattern as a regex and skips the sentinel write block
Bare '/allow Write /path' syntax discards the path as a comment, never producing a scoped sentinel
ACs 1 and 2 test the matcher with hand-crafted sentinels using '/tmp/allowed.json' — a path that cannot be produced via the real /allow writer path
critical_issues: hooks/userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:86-88: when flag_pattern is set via --tool, is_regex = _looks_regex(flag_pattern) must NOT be called — --tool flag is an explicit literal declaration. Fix: set is_regex=False unconditionally when flag_pattern is not None

hooks/userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh:90-97: bare '/allow Write /path' treats '/path' as comment. Fix: special-case pattern splitting to detect 'Write <path>' form and emit entry['target'] = path, OR document and enforce that '--tool' is the required syntax for scoped Write grants

Add end-to-end AC: '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/file.json"' → userprompt-consent-allowlist.sh invoked → sentinel file has target='/tmp/file.json' → match_sentinel_grant_for_write allows /tmp/file.json → denies /tmp/other.json
refined_context.recommended_approach: BA should re-analyze with expanded scope: (1) fix '--tool' literal enforcement (is_regex=false when flag_pattern set), (2) decide on bare 'Write <path>' handling, (3) add end-to-end AC covering the full user path with a dotted filename. The matcher fix (key-presence branching, exact equality, fail-closed on malformed entries) is correct and should be retained as-is. The writer fix is also correct in principle — just unreachable without the regex-skip fix.
refined_context.additional_context: The key insight from live testing: '/allow --tool "Write /tmp/allowed.json"' (the exact paths used in AC1 and AC2) produces IS_REGEX=true and writes NO sentinel. This is because the flag_pattern 'Write /tmp/allowed.json' contains '.' which _looks_regex() treats as a regex metacharacter. The sentinel write block at lines 180-182 exits early. The privilege escalation therefore persists unaffected for all real-world filenames with extensions, even after the writer fix lands.
